The FIDIC Stall Didn’t Need Another Legal Letter.
It Needed a Reframe.

How one question broke a €400M power plant dispute — and what it means for any contract that has gone positional.

Serguei Poppeleer  ·  Founder, Contracks Global  ·  contracks.global

FIDIC Silver Book · Contractor’s Claim · Delay Analysis · Legal Letters — and one question that changed the conversation.

€40,000 a day.

That is what a stalled construction contract costs when both sides stop talking about the project and start talking about their positions.

A major Belgian energy provider needed a new power plant. The base contract was FIDIC Silver Book — a framework designed for EPC turnkey delivery. Over months of negotiation, both sides had amended almost every clause until the original FIDIC language was barely recognisable. By the time legal review was complete, a flowchart was needed just to explain how a single payment adjustment mechanism worked. What had started as a straightforward formula had become a multi-layered sequence of conditions, approvals, and qualifications that filled several pages and fed hours of late-night discussion.

Then geopolitics intervened. The price jumped from €325M to €400M before a signature had been placed. The contractor had been bidding against expectations that no longer existed. The client had given no guidance on where the price ceiling sat. Both sides arrived at the table with hardened positions, a contract neither team fully understood, and a gap nobody knew how to close.

By week three, the only communication happening between parties was through legal counsel and revised claim notices.

“When both sides are talking about their positions instead of the project, the project has already lost.”

I was brought in not to argue the position — but to break the stall.

The assumption that keeps disputes expensive

The standard assumption when a FIDIC dispute hardens: this is a legal problem. Better arguments needed. More precedent. Stronger letters. A more senior legal team.

It is rarely true.

In this case, the contract itself had become part of the problem. Every clause had been negotiated so heavily that neither side had a shared operating framework to stand on. The flowchart that was supposed to clarify the adjustment mechanism had instead become evidence of how far the parties had drifted from a workable commercial relationship.

Legal confirms that each side is right. That is precisely the problem. Two parties, both legally correct on their reading of a contract nobody fully agreed on, both commercially stuck, both watching the clock.

The contract had created the positions. It could not resolve them. Something else had to.

This is the same pattern that appears across delivery governance. Documents create records. They do not create alignment. Contracts define positions. They do not create commercial consensus. In both cases, the paperwork survives while the project drifts.

When that happens, the problem is no longer contractual. It is operational.

What broke the stall

Both teams in separate rooms. Every additional day carried a direct cost while commissioning moved further away and senior management attention remained trapped in the dispute. The intervention was not a negotiation. It was a diagnosis.

Before anyone sat across a table, each party was asked one question — separately, without the other present:

“If this were resolved tomorrow — what would that actually look like for you?”

Not “what are your claims?” Not “what will you accept?” What would resolution look like.

The answers were different in language. They were nearly identical in substance.

The contractor needed price certainty — a mechanism that acknowledged the geopolitical movement in costs and gave them a defensible basis for the €400M figure. The client needed the programme commitment preserved and a contract structure they could take to their board without explaining a €75M increase against an expectation that had never been formally set.

Neither of those were incompatible. Weeks of legal letters had made them look that way. The flowchart that had caused so much confusion became, in the mapping session, the most useful document on the table — not as a contractual instrument, but as a visual demonstration of why neither side could clearly see where the other was standing.

The overlap was the deal. It just needed to be visible to both sides at the same time.

A structured conversation followed — not a negotiation, a mapping exercise. What each side actually needed. What each side could offer. Where those things connected.

The stall broke in two sessions. Not because anyone changed the contract. Not because one side surrendered its position. The conversation simply shifted from defending claims to protecting project outcomes.

The project moved to signature. The commercial relationship survived. The legal costs stopped accruing. Both sides moved forward with a contract that — still heavily amended — at least had a commercial logic both parties understood.

That outcome was not achieved by a better legal argument. It was achieved by changing what the conversation was about.

The Commercial Reset™

The pattern has since become a repeatable five-step reset sequence. A stalled contract does not need more legal horsepower. It needs a different frame.

Step 1
Separate the parties

Stop putting both sides in the same room. Positional conversations harden in joint sessions — each party performs for their team, their lawyers, their management chain. Separate the conversations before attempting to reconnect them.

Step 2
Diagnose interests, not positions

Ask each party what resolution actually looks like. Not what they are claiming. Not what they will accept. What the project needs to look like in six months for this to have been resolved well. Listen for the commercial interest underneath the contractual position.

Step 3
Map the overlap

In almost every stall, the underlying interests are more compatible than the stated positions. Map them side by side. The gap between “what we need” and “what they need” is almost always smaller than the gap between the claim schedules — and far smaller than the gap between the lawyers.

Step 4
Reframe the room

Bring both parties back together around the project — not around the dispute. The agenda is not “who owes what.” The agenda is “what does a successful project outcome require from each side?” That shift changes the entire dynamic of the conversation. The flowchart that had paralysed two rooms became, in a reframed session, a shared tool for finding the path.

Step 5
Commercial agreement first. Legal expression second.

Once the reframe holds, move fast. Agree the principle. Document it. Let the legal teams work out the contractual expression of what both sides have agreed commercially. That sequence — commercial agreement first, legal expression second — is the opposite of how most disputes are managed. It works faster and at a fraction of the cost.

The objections I hear before every reset

“The other side isn’t acting in good faith.”

Usually, they are. What looks like bad faith is almost always a party protecting a position their legal team has confirmed is solid. The moment the conversation moves from positions to interests, the bad faith tends to disappear. In heavily amended contracts particularly, what looks like aggression is often confusion — both sides reacting to a framework neither fully understands.

“We have a strong legal position. Why would we reframe?”

A strong legal position is a negotiating asset — not a reason to avoid a faster, cheaper resolution that preserves the project and the relationship. Arbitration often takes years, consumes executive attention, and permanently changes the commercial relationship. A position that wins in arbitration but loses the contract is not a win.

“The contract forces us to follow the formal dispute process.”

FIDIC has a dispute process. It does not prevent commercial conversations happening in parallel. The formal process is the backstop. The reset happens in the space between the current position and arbitration — a space that both sides have every incentive to use, especially when the contract itself has become unworkable through over-amendment.

The same principle — two more proof cases

That Belgian stall was not the only time this held.

On a cross-border gas transmission project in France — GRT Gaz — a six-month commercial deadlock was broken using the same sequence. Separate diagnosis. Interest mapping. Reframe from positions to project value. The deadlock that had consumed six months of senior management time resolved in three sessions.

On the Baltic Pipe — €850M+, cross-border, hostile contracting environment — the commercial position was maintained through a period of intense contractual pressure not by winning legal arguments, but by keeping the conversation anchored to project outcomes rather than the letter of an amended contract.

The moment both sides start talking about what the project needs instead of what the contract says, a path forward exists. It always existed. The positional framing just made it invisible.

Different sectors. Different scales. Different FIDIC books — some more recognisable than others. Same principle.
